comment_text.sn15.1:1.1: This chapter links to the Nidānasaṁyutta through the concept of beginningless transmigration, in which beings are lost because of not understanding dependent origination (SN 12.60:2.3).
comment_text.sn15.1:1.7: Resolve anamatagga as ana’mat’agga, where ana is a doubled negative: “no known beginning”. | The idea stems from the the Nāsadīya Sūkta, which twice mentions “in the beginning” (agre, Rig Veda 10.129.3, 4; see too 10.90.7, 10.121.1). There, from thought (manaso) evolved desire. It concludes, ”This creation—from where it came to be, if it was produced or if not—he who is the overseer of this (world) in the furthest heaven, he surely knows. Or if he does not know …?” (Rig Veda 10.129.7, Jamison/Brereton). Perhaps the Buddha learned the phrase from his former teacher Uddaka Rāmaputta, who at Isibhāsiyāiṁ 23 describes transmigration as aṇādīyaṁ aṇavadaggaṁ (“beginningless, without first point”).
comment_text.sn15.1:1.8: We encountered a slight variation of this phrase at SN 12.19:1.2.
comment_text.sn15.1:1.9: The “Black Plum Tree Land” is jambudīpa, the South Asian subcontinent, including India, Pakistan, Nepal, Bangladesh, and Afghanistan.

root_text.sn15.1:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā sāvatthiyaṁ viharati jetavane anāthapiṇḍikassa ārāme.
root_text.sn15.1:1.8: Pubbā koṭi na paññāyati avijjānīvaraṇānaṁ sattānaṁ taṇhāsaṁyojanānaṁ sandhāvataṁ saṁsarataṁ.
root_text.sn15.1:1.9: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, puriso yaṁ imasmiṁ jambudīpe tiṇakaṭṭhasākhāpalāsaṁ taṁ chetvā ekajjhaṁ saṁharitvā caturaṅgulaṁ caturaṅgulaṁ ghaṭikaṁ katvā nikkhipeyya:
root_text.sn15.1:1.10: ‘ayaṁ me mātā, tassā me mātu ayaṁ mātā’ti, apariyādinnāva, bhikkhave, tassa purisassa mātumātaro assu, atha imasmiṁ jambudīpe tiṇakaṭṭhasākhāpalāsaṁ parikkhayaṁ pariyādānaṁ gaccheyya.
root_text.sn15.1:1.13: Pubbā koṭi na paññāyati avijjānīvaraṇānaṁ sattānaṁ taṇhāsaṁyojanānaṁ sandhāvataṁ saṁsarataṁ.
root_text.sn15.1:1.14: Evaṁ dīgharattaṁ vo, bhikkhave, dukkhaṁ paccanubhūtaṁ tibbaṁ paccanubhūtaṁ byasanaṁ paccanubhūtaṁ, kaṭasī vaḍḍhitā.
root_text.sn15.1:1.15: Yāvañcidaṁ, bhikkhave, alameva sabbasaṅkhāresu nibbindituṁ alaṁ virajjituṁ alaṁ vimuccitun”ti.

translation_text.sn15.1:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
translation_text.sn15.1:1.3: There the Buddha addressed the mendicants,
translation_text.sn15.1:1.7: “Mendicants, this transmigration has no known beginning.
translation_text.sn15.1:1.8: No first point is found of sentient beings roaming and transmigrating, shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving.
translation_text.sn15.1:1.9: Suppose a person was to strip all the grass, sticks, branches, and leaves in the Black Plum Tree Land, gather them together into one pile, and chop them each into four inch pieces. They’d lay them down, saying:
translation_text.sn15.1:1.10: ‘This is my mother, this is my grandmother.’ The grass, sticks, branches, and leaves of the Black Plum Tree Land would run out before that person’s mothers and grandmothers.
translation_text.sn15.1:1.12: This transmigration has no known beginning.
translation_text.sn15.1:1.13: No first point is found of sentient beings roaming and transmigrating, shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving.
translation_text.sn15.1:1.14: For such a long time you have undergone suffering, agony, and disaster, swelling the cemeteries.
translation_text.sn15.1:1.15: This is quite enough for you to become disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding all conditions.”

variant_text.sn15.1:1.9: taṁ chetvā → tacchetvā (bj, sya-all, pts1ed, pts2ed)
